points: The Earth rotates on its own axis (through the North and South Poles) from West to East in approximately 24 hours; this causes the day-night cycle and the apparent motion of the Sun, Moon, and stars from East to West across the sky.

The Pole Star (Dhruva Tara) appears nearly stationary because the Earth's axis points close to it; all other stars appear to revolve around it — their apparent circular motion can be captured as star trails in long-exposure photographs.

Aryabhata (5th century CE) correctly recognised the Earth's rotation as the cause of the apparent daily motion of celestial objects, and calculated the period of one rotation as ~23 hours 56 minutes 4.1 seconds — close to the accepted value today.

summary.category: Revolution of the Earth and Seasons
points: The Earth revolves around the Sun in a nearly circular orbit, completing one revolution in ~365 days 6 hours; as the Earth orbits, different constellations become visible in the night sky over the course of a year.

Seasons are caused by the tilt of Earth's axis (not by Earth's distance from the Sun) combined with Earth's spherical shape — the hemisphere tilted towards the Sun receives more intense sunlight for a longer duration, causing summer there and winter in the opposite hemisphere.

Key dates in the Northern Hemisphere: Summer Solstice (~21 June, longest day), Winter Solstice (~22 December, shortest day), Spring Equinox (~21 March) and Autumn Equinox (~23 September) — both with 12 hours of day and night; seasons are reversed in the Southern Hemisphere.

A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon comes between the Sun and Earth (Sun → Moon → Earth); the Moon can cover the Sun because their apparent sizes are nearly equal — the Moon is ~400× smaller but also ~400× closer; a total solar eclipse is visible from only a small area for a few minutes and must never be viewed with the naked eye.

A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon (Sun → Earth → Moon), casting Earth's shadow on the Moon; during a total lunar eclipse the Moon appears dark red; lunar eclipses are visible from the entire night side of Earth and are safe to watch with the naked eye.

Ancient Indian texts such as the Surya Siddhanta contain calculations to predict eclipses; M. K. Vainu Bappu, the father of modern Indian astronomy, studied solar eclipses and established major observatories in India.
